package. It takes one argument — the initial value for the state variable — and returns an array of exactly two elements: the current state value and a setter function.
always returns the same pair in the same order, and destructuring gives them meaningful names.

content.text: . React uses it to seed the state slot on the first render. On every subsequent render, React ignores the argument and returns the current value from its internal store instead.
The initial value can be any JavaScript type:

const [count, setCount]     = useState(0);          // number
const [name, setName]       = useState('');          // string
const [open, setOpen]       = useState(false);       // boolean
const [items, setItems]     = useState([]);          // array
const [user, setUser]       = useState(null);        // null
const [error, setError]     = useState(undefined);   // undefined
const [pos, setPos]         = useState({ x: 0, y: 0 }); // object
The initial value expression is evaluated on every render, even though React only
the result on the first render. If computing it is expensive (e.g. parsing a large object from
and React will call it only on the first render:
The function receives no arguments and must return the initial value. React detects that you passed a function rather than a value and defers the call.
React tracks state by call order, not by name. You could write
and it would work. The universal convention is
— the setter name is "set" followed by the value name with an uppercase first letter.
has a stable identity — it never changes between renders. You can safely pass it to child components, store it in a
dependency array, or include it in a
dependency array without triggering extra effects.
The setter accepts two call forms:

setCount(5);          // state becomes 5
setName('Alice');      // state becomes 'Alice'
setOpen(true);         // state becomes true
Use this form when the new value does not depend on the previous value — when you know the exact next state up front.

setCount(prev => prev + 1);          // increment
setOpen(prev => !prev);               // toggle
setItems(prev => [...prev, newItem]); // append

Use this form when the new value depends on the previous value. React calls the function with the most recent state at the time the update is applied, even if multiple updates are queued. This prevents stale-closure bugs when multiple setState calls happen before a re-render.
Multiple useState Calls in One Component
as many times as needed. Each call creates a completely independent state slot. React identifies them by their call order, so they must appear in the same order on every render.
Because React identifies state slots by call order,
At the top level of the component function
— not inside loops, conditions, or nested functions.
Only inside a React function component or a custom Hook
— not in regular helper functions, classes, or module-level code.
package enforces these rules automatically in your editor and CI pipeline.
If you call the setter with the same value the state already holds, React bails out and does not re-render the component. It uses
This is why you must always produce a new object or array when updating state that holds a reference type. Mutating in place leaves the reference identical and React skips the update.
TypeScript infers the state type from the initial value. When the inference is too narrow or you need a union type, provide a type argument explicitly:
The type constraint is enforced on every call to the setter: calling
When several state variables always change together, group them into a single object rather than declaring them separately. This reduces the number of
calls and keeps the relationship explicit:

Conversely, do not group variables that change independently. Mixing unrelated state in one object means the entire object must be spread on every update — and if one value changes, all subscribers to that state object re-render even if they only read the unchanged values.
Extracting State into a Custom Hook
When the same state logic appears in multiple components, extract it into a custom Hook. Custom Hooks are plain functions whose names start with
state — custom Hooks do not share state between callers, just logic.
React error: "Rendered more hooks than during the previous render"

Choosing Between One Object and Multiple Variables
There is no single rule. Here is a practical heuristic:
when the values are unrelated and change independently — e.g.,
when the values are tightly coupled — e.g., a position

State Initialisation Patterns in Real Projects
In production code, the initial state value often comes from one of three sources:
components where the parent provides an initial value but does not own the state. Name the prop with an
or a cookie via a lazy initializer to avoid re-reading every render:
Read the initial filter or page from the URL when the component mounts. For truly URL-driven state, consider keeping the value
the URL with a router's search params API rather than mirroring it in
State declarations are an implementation detail — do not test them directly. Instead, test the
they enable: what the user sees and what happens after interactions.

The React DevTools browser extension lets you inspect state at runtime without adding
statements everywhere. Open DevTools → Components tab → select a component in the tree. The panel on the right shows:
in call order. You can edit the value live to see how the component responds.
Custom hook names are also visible in DevTools: a hook named
in the hooks list, grouping the state variables it owns under its name.

Sharing state across many components in a subtree
